data.title: High Profit And Low Debt Could Transform Headwater Exploration Into The Next Oil Star
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant Headwater Exploration (OTCPK:CDDRF) (TSX:HWX:CA) a Strong Buy rating. I think that the company can increase its revenues and earnings in the upcoming years. The first aspect is its high EBIT margin of 47.58%, considerably higher than the margins reported by its peers. As such, the company can use its revenues to expand its operations. In addition, it anticipates producing 26,500 barrels of oil equivalent ((boe/d)) in 2027, representing a 30.48% increase from 2024. This year, it expects to pay a dividend yield of 6.35%. Another relevant aspect is that Headwater has a low debt level. For instance, in Q1 2025, it reported a 25.22% liabilities-to-assets ratio. Furthermore, its management team has been successful in the oil industry.

The WTI forward curve suggests lower oil prices for the upcoming years. This curve indicates that the WTI spot price could drop to values in the range of $59.7 to $63.3 per barrel in the upcoming years. If this scenario occurs, my estimation is that the operating margin of Headwater could fall to 40.13%. On the other hand, the OPEC+ expects to add more than 2 million barrels of oil this year, thereby increasing the global oil supply. With more oil in the market, the oil prices could decline, negatively affecting Headwater's revenues. When pondering all the positive and negative aspects of Headwater, I still consider the company to be an interesting investment opportunity.

The Company

Headwater is an oil and gas company headquartered in Calgary, Alberta, Canada. In Q1 2025, its heavy oil production accounted for 88% of the total output, while natural gas and liquefied natural gas represented the remaining 12%, as depicted in the following chart. Its business strategy focuses on assets that generate attractive long-term returns while maintaining low debt levels.

History

The transformation of Headwater began in 2020. That year, the company restructured its management team. In addition, it acquired the assets of Cenovus (CVE) located in the Marten Hills area of Alberta, Canada. Those assets consisted of producing 2,800 boe/d of heavy oil that year. For this transaction, Headwater paid a total consideration of approximately CA$135.3 million. The same year, it changed its management team. Thanks to these decisions, Headwater increased its production from 620 boe/d in 2019 to 7,393 boe/d in 2021. To provide context, Martens Hills averaged 6,785 boe/d in 2021. That year, Headwater invested CA$140 million in capital expenditures.

Moving to 2022, Headwater expanded its operations to Greater Peavine and West Nipisi, located in Alberta, Canada. In those places, it acquired CA$33 million of land. Due to these acquisitions and the significant investments in Capex, Headwater's production reached an average of 22,066 boe/d in Q1 2025.

Headwater achieved this result by issuing common shares. In 2019, it had 88,147 common shares, increasing to 237,774 in Q1 2025, representing a 169.75% increase. Further, the company has employed its own resources to expand its operations. In 2024, it reported an operating profit of CA$242.18 million and a capex of CA$133.82 million. These figures indicate that Headwater has significant resources to expand its operations.

Low Debt

Debt is an important aspect to evaluate in any company. There are companies with excellent growth performance, but they finish in bankruptcy due to their high debt. As such, I think that analyzing Headwater's debt will enhance the analysis presented in this article.

In Q1 2025, Headwater reported cash and cash equivalents of CA$125.55 million and total current assets of CA$187.48 million. Meanwhile, in the same quarter, it had current liabilities of CA$115.38 million and non-current liabilities of CA$128.65 million, as depicted in the following chart. In other words, Headwater can pay its current liabilities and part of its non-current liabilities only using its cash and cash equivalents. Furthermore, the company had a 25.22% liabilities-to-assets ratio in Q1 2025. This ratio indicates that the company has low debt levels.

Headwater has significant financial resources to meet its obligations. As such, even if oil prices decline, the company will have resources to pay its liabilities. Ultimately, this solid financial position reduces the risk of bankruptcy. With a low bankruptcy risk, Headwater's stock price is less likely to experience a sharp decline. In other words, there is more upside potential than downside risk.

Profits

Headwater has reported significant net profit margins, as depicted in the chart below. Since 2020, its net income margin has been above 28%. In the first quarter of 2025, the WTI oil price averaged $71.78 per barrel, 7.38% lower than its average in the same quarter of 2024. Despite this decline, the company achieved a high net income margin of 36.81% that quarter.

This section would be incomplete without comparing Headwater with other Canadian oil and gas companies. The following table shows some relevant profit ratios of Headwater, Baytex Energy (BTE), ARC Resources (OTCPK:AETUF), Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), CVE, Ovintiv (OVV), Strathcona Resources (OTCPK:STHRF), Vermilion Energy (VET), Suncor Energy (SU), Whitecap Resources (OTCPK:WCPRF), Obsidian Energy (OBE), and Bonterra Energy (OTCPK:BNEFF). I selected a large list of companies to highlight that Headwater has the highest profitability ratios. For instance, it has a 76.44% gross profit margin, followed by BTE, which has a 69.07% gross profit margin. Additionally, its EBIT margin is 47.58%, significantly higher than the values reported by its peers. Finally, the return on equity of Headwater is almost three times the median value of the peer group. Due to these excellent ratios, the company has achieved outstanding financial results.

Management

In 2020, Headwater underwent a management team change, which significantly transformed its operations. There are two key figures in this new team. The first is its executive chairman, Neil Roszell, and the second is its CEO, Jason Jaskela. Both were founders of Raging River Exploration and Wild Stream Exploration.

In the Sedar, I found that Ranging River produced 5,665 boe/d in 2013, increasing to 22,867 boe/d in 2017. Furthermore, Ranger River achieved net income margins of 8.67% and 14.91% in 2016 and 2017, respectively. The company was profitable despite the fact that the WTI oil price averaged $43.29 per barrel in 2016 and $50.80 per barrel in 2017. For instance, in 2024, the WTI crude oil price averaged $76.63 per barrel. I include these numbers to highlight that Headwater's CEO and executive chairman have a proven track record of success in managing oil companies.

Outlook and Revenues

For 2025, the company anticipates a 10% increase in its production. With that increase, it will produce 22,250 boe/d, as depicted in the following chart. To reach this goal, Headwater plans to invest CA$225 million in capital expenditures. In addition, it forecasts to produce 26,500 boe/d in 2027, up 30.48% more compared to 2024.

To enhance the analysis, the following table shows the revenue and EBITDA growth rates of Headwater, BTE, AETUF, CNQ, CVE, OVV, STHRF, VET, SU, WCPRF, OBE, and BNEFF. These companies are relevant oil and natural gas producers in Canada. In the last five years, Headwater reported a CAGR of 150.31% in its revenues, the highest growth by a large margin among its peers. Meanwhile, its EBITDA grew at 24.70% over a three-year period, also the highest value among its peers. To conclude, the company has reported higher growth metrics than its peers.

Dividends

Headwater also pays dividends, which is a positive aspect. This year, the analyst consensus expects a dividend yield of 6.35%, the second-highest value among its peers, as depicted in the following chart. In addition, it paid an average dividend yield of 3.27% in the last four years.

Valuation

In this section, I use the relative valuation method to estimate the fair value per share of Headwater. For this purpose, I implement the relative valuation technique to the following ratios: price-to-earnings, EV/EBITDA, and price-to-book ratio. The companies to use in the analysis are BTE, AETUF, CNQ, CVE, OVV, STHRF, VET, SU, and WCPRF. With these firms, I estimate the median value of each ratio.

To begin with, I use the median as a target ratio. When implementing the relative valuation technique to the price-to-earnings ratio, the fair value of Headwater is $6.94 per share, as depicted in the table below. At that price, Headwater is undervalued by 37.67%. Meanwhile, for the EV/EBITDA ratio, the relative valuation method suggests that $5.56 is the fair value per share of Headwater, pointing out that the company is undervalued by 10.29%. Finally, the relative valuation applied to the price-to-book ratio suggests that Headwater is overvalued by 48.30%. This is the only ratio that suggests an overvaluation.

Up to this point in the article, Headwater has paid more dividends. In addition, it has higher profit ratios than its peers. Two out of three relative valuations indicate that the company is undervalued compared to its peers. I believe that these findings are signs that Headwater presents a significant investment opportunity. As Warren Buffett said:

It's far better to buy a wonderful company at a fair price than a fair company at a wonderful price.

I think that Headwater ascribes to the previous quote. The relative valuation techniques implemented in this section suggest that the company is slightly undervalued. Headwater is also a wonderful business with an excellent profit margin and a fast growth rate.

Price Analysis

The following chart illustrates that Headwater has experienced sharp declines in its stock price in different periods. In addition, from 2007 to 2019, its stock price lost most of its value. I am of the opinion that the high volatility that the company has experienced still affects Headwater. Many investors may be deterred from investing in Headwater due to its poor previous performance.

However, the company has radically changed since 2020. For instance, in 2018, its operations were concentrated on extracting natural gas. Meanwhile, in 2024, its operations were primarily focused on oil extraction. Furthermore, the company has a new management team. These factors lead me to believe that if oil prices do not experience a sharp decline, the company's stock price will not experience a drastic drop in the upcoming years.

Reserves

I think that Headwater has low oil and gas reserve levels compared to other Canadian companies, which is a negative aspect. In 2024, Headwater had a proven reserve life index of 4.7, as depicted in the table below. In other words, at the production level of 2024, its proved reserves will last for 4.7 years. Furthermore, the company had proved and probable reserves of 7.4 years. For instance, in 2024, CNQ said that it has reserves for 33 years and proved plus probable reserves for 43 years. Additionally, VET had 9 years of proved reserves and 14 years of proved plus probable reserves in 2024.

Oil Outlook and Operating Profit Margin

The oil market expects lower prices for the upcoming years. In my experience analyzing the commodity market, the forward curve is a useful tool for gaining insight into the price of a particular commodity over the month and year ahead. For instance, the WTI future price of this month trades at $66.37 per barrel, as depicted in the chart below. Meanwhile, the WTI future price of May 2026 is $62.43 per barrel. As such, from now to May 2026, the WTI barrel of oil is expected to decline by $3.94. On the other hand, the forward curve chart indicates that the WTI oil prices will range from $59.7 per barrel to $63.3 per barrel between 2027 and 2036. Based on that range, I think that $61 per barrel is a reasonable assumption as the long-term price of this commodity for the upcoming years.

With the previous chart and Headwater's financial information, it is possible to derive the company's expected operating margin. In the following table, I calculated Headwater's operating costs per boe, highlighted in red. For example, in 2024, the operating costs to produce a barrel of oil were $27.19, while the WTI oil price averaged $76.63 per barrel. I used the annual average exchange rate between the Canadian dollar and the U.S. dollar to transform the operating costs to U.S. dollars. Hypothetically, if Headwater had sold its boe production at the WTI oil price, it would have reported an operating profit margin of 65.24% in 2024. If the WTI price declines to $61, this metric will decline to 55.43%, representing a 15.04% decrease compared to the value of 2024.

Turning to Headwater, the last column of the table displays the company's actual operating profit margin. If I assume that Headwater's operating profit margin declines by 15.04%, it will fall to 40.13%. This value represents the operating profit margin that Headwater may achieve if the WTI price declines to $61 per barrel, which is the expected long-term WTI price.

OPEC+

On July 5, 2025, OPEC+ agreed to increase its oil production by 548,000 barrels per day. For this year, the OPEC+ plans to increase its production by 1.918 million barrels of oil. Additionally, the oil organization anticipates adding another 580,000 barrels of oil per day this year. As such, this year the global oil supply will experience a significant increase, which could push oil prices down. If this occurs, Headwater's revenues will be negatively affected.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I consider Headwater to be an excellent investment opportunity. To begin with, the company has a management team that has been successful in the oil industry. In addition, it has low debt levels, which indicates that it can resist a period of low oil prices. It also has a high EBIT margin of 47.58%, significantly higher than those of its peers. Furthermore, it expects to produce 26,500 boe/d in 2027, an increase of 30.48% compared to 2024. The company also expects to pay 6.35% in dividends this year, another positive aspect.

Headwater has low levels of proven reserves, which is a negative factor. It has proved reserves for 4.7 years. To provide context, CNQ has proved reserves for 33 years. Further, the WTI forward curve suggests lower oil prices in the upcoming years. My estimation suggests that if the WTI oil price declines to $61 per barrel, the operating expenses of the companies could decline by 15.04%. Additionally, the OPEC+ expects to add more than 2 million barrels of oil to the market this year. The significant increase in the oil supply could push oil prices down. When taking all of these factors into consideration, I still believe that Headwater represents an interesting investment opportunity.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Companhia Siderurgica Nacional: The Deleveraging Asymmetry Play
data.description:
Companhia Siderúrgica Nacional (NYSE:SID), also known under the acronym "CSN", is one of the largest steel producers in Brazil, as well as the second largest iron ore producer, behind the giant Vale SA (VALE) in the country.

The company's investment thesis is based on its capital-intensive model and dictated by its sensitivity to the prices of the commodities it exports, as well as a high exposure to FX. In recent years, the company has suffered from the fall in iron ore prices and a gradual but slow recovery in steel prices, very pressured by uncertainties over the ongoing tariff war. The unfavorable flow of investment into emerging markets such as Brazil has also hurt. On top of this, CSN has suffered from high leverage and negative free cash flows—points I highlighted in my previous coverage, where I adopted a neutral stance on the ADR.

That said, with performance virtually flat throughout this year, at least stemming the bloodletting that took place in 2024 when SID devalued by more than 60%, I believe that the results posted in 1Q25 may be signaling a deleveraging cycle that could prove very pricey for CSN over the coming months.

Although the projections for iron ore prices are still not necessarily encouraging for the course of this year, in an eventual scenario of interest rate cuts in the U.S. and potentially in Brazil, with CSN doing its homework in favor of efficiency control and deleveraging, the path tends to be towards a reduction in interest expenses and falling leverage, making room for free cash flow generation.

Trading at EBITDA multiples almost 50% below its global peers, and well below its book value, although the discount is justified by the high country risk involved in the thesis, I believe that this discrepancy tends to fall sharply in a scenario of deleveraging, even if it is gradual. That's why I see an asymmetry in CSN's thesis, and I'm upgrading my rating from Hold to Buy—going against the sell-side consensus.

Commodity Cycle and Currency Impact on CSN

First of all, CSN is a highly cyclical company, since it operates in steel and mining (mainly iron ore), which are directly linked to global economic cycles. Because of this, the performance of its ADR tends to have a high correlation with two factors that I see as key being (1) commodity prices, especially iron ore, steel, and their derivatives; and (2) FX based on being an exporting company from Brazil, if the USD rises, this helps revenues in BRL.

The graph below shows that, today, SID has a correlation of 0.85 to iron ore spot prices, which denotes a very high sensitivity to commodity swings—albeit with some ups and downs in this correlation over the last twelve months. For example, in the last twelve months, SID has plummeted from $2.40 to $1.44, in similar proportions as iron ore has fallen from $108 USD/t to ~$92 USD/t.

In terms of steel, the HRC steel price at $875 USD/t has shown a certain stability throughout this year, after strong rises between Jan/Mar 2025 (from ~700 to ~950). Although CSN's main driver is iron ore, HRC remains an indirect proxy as the company sells flat steel in Brazil and also exports. And the firm HRC price guarantees healthy margins.

The last but not least point is with regard to FX, as today 1 USD equals 5.58 BRL—despite a peak of 6.40BRL earlier this year. Specifically for CSN, the weaker BRL is, in theory, advantageous, as exports tend to yield more in BRL, from where the company's costs originate.

But as CSN's ADR is traded in USD, if the BRL weakens too much, market risk sentiment tends to worsen, and so foreign investors price more risk into Brazil as a whole—risk-free rates are high, and capital flows out. However, with the macro scenario showing a slight perception of lower risk over the course of this year—as shown by Brazilian equities rallying in 2025—the inversely proportional correlation has been reversed over the past few months.

So, I would argue that CSN has spent the last twelve months in a situation of double pressure (1) with iron ore falling ~15% in that period and (2) with the USD falling since the peak, reducing the "natural hedge" of exports. The counterpart is HRC steel, holding up the price.

The market has penalized CSN's ADR more than iron ore alone would justify. So, I see part of the sharpest fall in this period as extra risk premiums based on company-related issues such as (1) high leverage, and (2) competition in the domestic market.

CSN Recent Developments

After a clearer view of the macroeconomic factors affecting CSN, its 1Q25 results have been choppy QoQ but actually decent YoY.

Starting with the high points, CSN is coming off record mining sales, up 5.4% year on year, still demonstrating solid demand, reflected in revenue 2.1% higher than the same period last year, despite iron ore prices still under strong pressure. Mining EBITDA advanced by 26% year-over-year, with a margin of 41%, reaching R$1.4 billion at a more efficient C1 Cost of $21/t, which is 11% lower than the same period last year.

As steel prices have appreciated over the last twelve months, steel sales grew by 8% year-over-year, causing the segment's EBITDA to more than double to R$485 million, with margins of 7.9% versus 4.3% in 1Q24.

The other less significant segments, cement, logistics, and energy, boosted the consolidated EBITDA result to R$2.5 billion, with a margin of 22%, an increase of 28% compared to the same period in 2024.

The problem is that in the quarterly comparison, EBITDA came in 24.8% lower, most notably dragged down by mining, steel, and cement, which came in R$571 million, R$171 million, and R$145 million lower, respectively.

That said, it was not possible to convert this amount into free cash flow.

Subtracting from the R$2.5 billion in EBITDA, R$1.12 billion in CapEx, and a further R$1.2 billion in financial results (due to high leverage), along with income tax expenses and others, the adjusted free cash flow was negative at R$173 million.

The bright spot is that, at least, based on the bottom line improvements year-over-year, CSN still managed to reduce its gross debt by R$3.6 billion, which helped bring its gearing from previously 3.5x in 4Q24 to 3.3x in 1Q24.

What’s Next for CSN?

The expectation is that, over the course of 2025, just as it proved in 1Q25, the deleveraging path will continue more robustly and may converge in CSN's favor.

Because CSN is a capital-intensive company, in the event of a change in interest rates, leverage here tends to be an asset. At the moment, CSN's debt structure is ~66% in USD (with an average cost of 6.60% per year) and ~34% in BRL (tied to 117% of the CDI, i.e., a massive 17.4%).

In other words, the ideal mix to accelerate leverage here would be the Fed cutting interest rates + the Brazilian Central Bank starting to lower interest rates (Selic) by 15%. The trend in July 2025 is for cuts, not hikes, with the consensus for Fed Funds leaning towards 2-3 gradual cuts by the end of 2025. In Brazil, the cut bias is also a reality, but potentially slower and more limited, with Selic projections closing 2025 at the same 15%. So, I would say that the odds are very much in favor, in the current situation, of CSN deleveraging further over the course of 2025.

But of course, the scenario is not so simple. The commodities environment remains hostile, with trade wars likely to weigh on maintaining downward pressure on iron ore. Platts TSI 62% Fe CFR China has been priced as a consensus of between R$95 and $110/t for the year ended December 31, expecting a slight recovery in H2 if there are new stimuli in China. However, no one is betting on a return to $130-150/t, as in previous cycles.

As for the cost of extraction, C1/t, CSN, which historically has a higher cost than Vale SA—because the mines are not as large in scale, but are improving efficiency—from $20-$23/t versus $18-$20/t. But CSN's projections for 2025 remain at between ~$21/t to ~$23/t, and, in my view, it is unlikely to lower the cost more than that without expanding scale.

That said, I at least see CSN on the right track, doing its part, which is (1) looking for ways to deleverage in the face of monetary tightening and unfavorable commodity cycles; (2) diversifying its operations into logistics, energy, and diluting sectoral risks; (3) leveraging favorable exchange rates, while still being able to benefit from an appreciated USD against the BRL, as well as strict exchange rate and financial hedge control to protect margins and cash.

And then there's the question of dividends. As CSN's shareholder remuneration policy is flexible, the company also distributes dividends based on accumulated profit reserves, even when there are periods (such as the current one) when net profit is negative. For example, last year, in November, CSN approved the distribution of R$730 million in interim dividends, using its more than R$24 billion in cash reserves (at that time), which resulted in an average yield of 14.3% in 2024.

Over the course of 2025 alone, a further R$1.3 billion in dividends have been announced and are expected to be distributed by the end of the year, which today results in a yield of ~16%—quite high but also a reflection of the sharply falling share price over the last twelve months.

The management team has shown an interest in balancing the distribution of dividends with the need to reduce leverage, and that, as the company progresses in this area, the plan is to return to levels closer to R$1 billion per semester.

Of course, it's prudent to see these robust dividends as something potentially temporary, rather than a constant and secure flow. But it also seems quite attractive as leverage gives way to a reduction in the financial result (interest expenses), weighing on cash flows, and as the company returns to posting a positive free cash flow with greater stability.

A Quick Word Ahead of 2Q25 Earnings

That said, the next acid test for CSN is imminent. The company is preparing to report its 2Q25 earnings on July 31, and to beat market estimates, it will need to report EPS above $0.11—that is, a good improvement on the -$0.07 reported in 2Q24 and the -$0.10 reported in 1Q25. Revenues will need to come in above $1.99 billion, which would represent virtually no improvement on the same period last year, although it is slightly above the $1.93 billion reported last quarter.

According to management commentary, in the last earnings call, the commitment to further debt reduction was strongly emphasized, and that, with EBITDA cooperating, could bring a good chance of improvement in leverage ratios as early as 2Q25.

And although there was no explicit guidance for mining, the implication is that it is expected that in Q2, seasonality/weather would normalize, so record volumes would not be repeated. Therefore, the focus would be on cost discipline (C1 cost control), which doesn't change much from 1Q25.

Regarding steel, CSN commented that it expects to maintain the solid performance in the segment after raising prices in 1Q25 and mentioned that stable costs helped double EBITDA. However, I wouldn't be surprised if competitive dynamics (especially in long steel) could put pressure on margins versus last quarter. But overall, my view is for a sustained EBITDA quarter-over-quarter for steel.

Finally, with regard to the cement segment, the trend is for a recovery in 2Q25 driven by seasonality, pushing EBITDA close to historical levels again—this could be a pleasant surprise in consolidated EBITDA, resonating well with the deleveraging process.

All in all, the odds are against 2Q25 being a "super" quarter. I'd expect more of the same from 1Q25, but with year-over-year EBITDA growth, which should help post more exciting leverage metrics.

Risks and Final Remarks

Trading at 4.2x its forward EBITDA, and below its book value at 0.83x, it seems that the pessimism of the commodities cycle added to all the pressures brought by leverage seems at least to be embedded in the share price.

However, as I pointed out at the beginning of the article, the performance of CSN's ADR is highly correlated with iron ore prices, steel industry demands, and FX, potentially offsetting any operational or financial improvements made by CSN's management team.

But overall, it seems to me that, at penny stock levels, Companhia Siderurgica Nacional is somewhat de-risked in the face of a cycle that is becoming more and more conducive to deleveraging, and a virtual stability in commodity prices for the coming months. Defying the prevailing market view, which is largely bearish on Brazilian iron ore and steel companies, I see in SID a point of asymmetry for those who tolerate volatility and understand that the biggest risk—the leveraged balance sheet—can be gradually mitigated as CSN prioritizes deleveraging and adjusts its asset mix to reinforce cash.

data.title: QQQI: Sounds Like A Good Idea But It Isn't Worth The Fees - Sell
data.description:
Introduction

The NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:QQQI) is a fund that seeks to deliver monthly income from investing in the constituents of the Nasdaq 100 by buying and selling options on the underlying index, NDX. This fund will allow investors to gain some upside with capital appreciation while also earning consistent income through its options strategy. The below are some of the key facts taken from the prospectus.

  • Expense Ratio: 0.68%

  • AUM: $3B

  • Distribution Frequency: monthly

  • Volume: 2M

Their top holdings are below.

Their top holdings are all top-tier companies in technology and AI, and we believe that these firms should continue to appreciate in time.

Top Holdings Analysis

Nvidia (NVDA): This GPU maker has been buoyed this week as a result of the lifting of the ban on cutting-edge AI chips for the Chinese market. Jensen Huang’s relationship management with the current administration has proven to be fruitful and he is currently in China forging new relationships and business connections. One of the best signs that the company is continuing to do well is the earnings report from their partner, Taiwan Semiconductor. Since they are the primary manufacturers of the chips that NVDA and AMD designs, the fact that their profits have surged 61% to record highs is a positive sign that the AI wave is only just still starting. We view this as highly bullish for a number of chip names, including NVDA. They are proving to still be the leader in the GPU space needed to power all of the most powerful AI models in the world, and as Taiwan Semiconductor’s earnings show, the insatiable demand is still very much present. As the company just powered through the $4T market cap barrier, the stock is priced for perfection, but so far into the AI development journey, we view NVDA as a star performer in this burgeoning field and believe that they will remain a top tier name to invest in. Therefore, we are pleased to see this fund have significant NVDA exposure.

Microsoft (MSFT): Microsoft remains a leader in the enterprise AI space and recently have been adjusting their workforce to better streamline themselves to further compete in AI development. Since they have announced their $80B investment funds this year on AI expenditures, the company has been busy investing into many use cases for AI. Their latest $4B investment focuses on a philanthropic effort to train teachers and students through AI learning, which helps the company with goodwill and training more future users. The below image shows their ramp in capex, much of it driven by AI, over the past few years.

Furthermore, as MSFT continues to build out massive data centers that require significant amounts of energy to operate, the firm has been buying large amounts of carbon credits to offset their impact on the environment - and this should allow MSFT to be a target of ESG funds and other ESG focused institutional money, which should help power MSFT to new highs.

Apple (AAPL): Apple has been viewed as a bit of a laggard in the big tech world when it comes to AI - and rightfully so, as the company does not appear to have a cohesive strategy compared to some of the other big tech players in the market. Shares have underperformed since the start of the year - see the below chart that compares AAPL to SPY and QQQ.

This ~25% delta is driven by the fact that investors are not confident in the direction that AAPL is taking when it comes to its AI investments. Their products have done a poor job so far of seamlessly incorporating AI into daily user flows, and they seem to have squandered their decade long lead of AI with Siri being more or less, useless. However, we view this hesitation and skepticism around Apple’s strategy as a long awaited opportunity to buy into one of the most dominant names in tech history. Sure, their AI strategy (or lack thereof) leaves something to be desired, but ultimately, their massive user base and fanatic loyalty of said users will continue to drive demand for their products with each new release. Speaking of which, we believe that since shares have been compressed to start the year, there will be a catalyst to help AAPL return to positive in the fall - and that is the launch of the latest iPhone. Now that all their end user devices are running the same OS number, we believe that is just the first step they are taking in developing a holistic, well rounded consumer AI strategy. We believe in Tim Cook’s leadership and view this dip in Apple as a phenomenal buying opportunity for a tremendous company.

Options strategy

QQQI employs an interesting options strategy where they will sell covered calls on “up to 100% of the value of the equity securities held by the fund” and then use part of the premium earned to buy out-of-the money call options on the same securities, also up to 100%. QQQI also states that the covered calls are generally sold at a strike that is close to the current price of NDX while the purchased calls will have a strike price higher than the current price. QQQI does not have a lot of detail beyond that on exactly how they are employing this strategy. However, the implication is that with proper management of the options being bought/sold, the managers of QQQI can generate a high income.

In order to assess the efficacy of such a strategy, we compare QQQI with QQQ, which is exactly the same as QQQI except they do not employ additional options trading, they only invest in NDX. The below chart shows the total returns of QQQI vs QQQ since QQQI was launched. Note: This chart shows “total returns” meaning that it also accounts for dividends.

As we see, the performance of both funds is very similar, with the total returns for the time period being 31.05% for QQQI and 34.80% for QQQ. It is concerning that QQQ is outperforming the ETF QQQI that was meant to generate higher returns. In fact, the overall returns for QQQ are higher than QQQI for most of the time period shown.

If we “zoom in” on the period between February 19th and April 8th (the big down trend in the above chart) we see that QQQI does outperform QQQ in that period, by 2.77%.

So we see that QQQI may be a good option compared to QQQ during negative market movements, but not by much. As the underlying Nasdaq-100 index is composed of high growth top tech names, we expect the overall trend to be positive which limits the benefit of QQQI.

Just for curiosity, we look at the performance comparison between QQQI and QQQ without dividends included:

In this view, QQQ very definitively outperforms QQQI. While this does not say too much about their relative performances because we are missing a big component from the dividends, this chart shows that QQQI consistently lags in performance and just barely catches up with the inclusion of dividends.

Based on the evidence so far, we do not see QQQI as a great option for investors. It would be simpler to just buy QQQ instead and avoid the additional complexity of the options for a higher return.

Risks

Rotation out of tech investing

There is a chance, despite minimal in our view, that investors rotate out of the AI trade that has been powering the markets for the past 18 months. Since the markets are indeed at all time highs, investors do have the right to be cautious about jumping in headfirst with so much bullishness everywhere. However, with a rate cut almost guaranteed to arrive in the fall, we take the view that markets should continue to appreciate going into that catalyst.

Management Fees

At 0.68%, the management fee for QQQI is quite high, even for an actively managed ETF. This means that investors are paying $6.80 per year for every $1000 invested. Which is much higher than the $2 you would pay by investing in QQQ instead for example. Based on the performance we have seen, we do not see 0.68% being worth it for investors. While $6.80 might seem like a low number, it will add up and compound over time, all for a sub-par performance result.

Conclusion

This fund tracks the Nasdaq 100, which results in it having exposure to some of the leading tech names in the market today. Companies like NVDA, MSFT, and AAPL are leaders in enterprise AI, consumer tech, cloud computing, and personal computing. These are some of the most quality companies in America, so we are pleased to see that this fund provides investors with exposure to some of these names.

However, based on the funds historical performance, we struggle to find a reason to invest in QQQI. It does not even outperform QQQ which tracks the same underlying equity but without the options trading. In addition a likely rotation out of AI/tech investments as tech stocks are at or near all time highs and the high management fees of QQQI add additional risk and performance degradations.

We like the idea of QQQI in general, and will be keeping a close eye on its performance in the future as past performance is not always an indicator of future performance. But until we see evidence of more value from QQQI, we rate it as a sell.

data.title: Netflix: Cash Flow Declines Again
data.description:
Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) reported earnings. As usual, the earnings and the margin expansion were pretty much as predicted. But also, as usual, cash flow failed to follow the earnings jump. The last article covers this, but the trend continues, which is worrying. That continues the warning signs for investors that cash flow may never get to 10% of the market value of the stock when this industry matures. Those of us from business school remember that earnings plus depreciation was a good estimate for operating cash flow (the GAAP number). Now that can be different here due to a slight difference in how this industry calculates the GAAP cash flow from operating activities. However, if it varies too much, then one needs to ask the "why?" question. Just like with people, when a big company sells something, investors have every right to expect the cash.

There are a couple of accounting issues here that need to be addressed that become apparent with an industry pioneer like this one when it reports earnings.

Sales Growth

The first is that sales (or revenue) grew roughly 5% over the previous quarter. The comparison made by management is in the teens. For many continuing businesses that are not industry pioneers, the market will assume that earnings will grow no faster than revenue as one cannot increase margin "forever." So even though that margin did increase over any other quarter shown above, many times the market considers that a one-time event.

That may not be the case here as this company has long promised a lot of cash flow in the future. But it never happened yet. However, as long as sales grow, then "hope springs eternal."

But the problem is that this company is already large. That usually means at some point the market switches gears and values this as a continuing business rather than a story stock. I personally do not want to be in this when that happens.

Margin

The second issue is that the margin increased. But cash flow did not increase in line with the margin or earnings.

Many times, industry pioneers like this one have considerable leeway to report earnings because there's no history and no competition. The past history of accounting is loaded with examples of accounting being reigned in after an industry is established and the investors have losses to prove it when the market decides to value the company differently.

Earnings

Here, earnings are more than both cash flow and free cash flow. But as an investor, you want to see the opposite. What can happen here is that the difference is now going to the balance sheet as an asset that may or may not have future value. Costs incurred are either expensed or capitalized and then amortized. Right now, the lower cash flow demonstrates that costs are being capitalized faster than they are amortized. With most businesses the opposite occurs. Here, capitalizing investments in the business as assets combined with the amortization assumptions is allowing earnings and the margin to grow faster than cash flow. How long this relationship continues is anyone's guess.

Understand a rapidly growing business may need cash flow to service working capital. But in this case revenue grew roughly 5% from the previous quarter and in the teens when compared to the previous fiscal year. That's generally not fast enough that working capital needs are big enough to decrease cash flow as is shown in the comparison (compared to the last quarter) or the very slow growth in cash flow in the second quarter compared to the first quarter comparison.

The percentage growth in cash flow over the last quarter in fiscal year 2024 was decent. It was something in the 88% range. But in the quarters since then cash flow has stalled at a very low percentage of the market value of the stock. The cash flow recently did not grow over the previous quarter.

This is a company that for the amount of business being done the cash flow is a very low percentage of sales and even lower percentage of market value of the stock. Typically, a 10% cash flow on market value would be expected except for fast growing companies. The 15% sales growth shown above (roughly) is decent. But it's not enough to account for the fact that the cash flow is a single-digit percentage of the market value of the stock. When the cash flow is that low, there should be big percentage increases in cash flow every quarter over the last quarter.

Meanwhile earnings have a bigger yield on both sales and market value.

Mr. Market

It's not unusual with a pioneer in a relatively new industry to choose accounting that backs the company storyline about future success and what it will look like.

But as an industry matures and growth slows, there's a demand for return of capital in place of the appreciation that's slowing (to a relative crawl). As the current yield on the market value of the stock easily demonstrates, this company is really in no position to return more than a nominal percentage of its market value to shareholders. Normally as a company matures, the demand is for something much greater than that.

Conclusion

One of the things to notice is that management focuses on revenue growth (customer growth) and earnings growth along with that margin. But the cash flow compared to the previous fiscal year barely crawled to a gain in comparison with the big margin and earnings gain. For investors, that should ring alarm bells as it may signal that costs are out of the control of management.

It may also mean there's some overestimating the future value of some capitalized assets. It would not be the first time that happened (usually by accident) among the companies I cover. It is in fact, a very common occurrence given all the write-offs I report about at market bottoms as management "clear the decks" waiting for a market recovery.

If this company were not a pioneer, then it would likely be valued at a multiple that takes into account revenue growth in the teens. But that implies a far lower stock price than is currently the case.

The price earnings ratio that can be justified by revenue growth would be somewhere in the teens at the most. But the cash flow yield should probably be at least 10% and maybe 15% which would lower the stock price even more.

Everything points to an overpriced stock because the market is focused upon an eventual large payday that has so far not happened. The danger of growth continuing to slow and the stock price being revalued as a regular company is climbing.

Now, when it comes to timing, I do not know when this will happen. But what I do know is when that revaluation occurs, it will likely wipe out several years of gains. So, if one chooses to "get out early" or even very early, that investor will likely have gains in this stock that many will not have if they hold until the end. For me, this remains a strong sell due to the stock price valuation.

Risks

There's a possibility that the cash flow situation reverses and cash flow growth exceeds earnings for a while to restore the relationship between earnings and cash flow that one normally sees.

It's also possible the company grows much larger and possibly faster than is the case right now.

The reason that may not happen is that the industry is entertainment and that industry is so fractured with different activities that no one player can dominate the whole area. Diversification is certainly possible. Netflix appears to be on a diversification "spree" to catch up with some competitors that are diversified. Whether that will resolve the cash flow issues is anyone's guess at the current time.

Netflix is profitable. As long as revenue continues to grow, the story of a "pot of gold at the end of the rainbow" remains alive. So, this stock could continue to be expensive for some time to come. There is the old saying "the market can stay irrational longer than you can stay solvent" that may apply here.

data.title: Lucid X Uber: The Alliance That Could Rewrite Autonomy
data.description:
Introduction

There are always many announcements in the AV space. Something always happens, but most of the time it doesn’t send a single stock flying double digits on the news announcement. But this one is different, and I must say, pretty exciting. It involves Uber, which potentially could be one of the strongest players in the AV industry. It involves real money, which means it’s not another future promise. It’s an actual project that has goals, deadlines, and an outcome we will be able to see very soon.

So, I am sure you have already heard by now, Uber Technologies, Inc. (NYSE:UBER) is partnering with Lucid Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:LCID) and Nuro (privately held) to launch over 20,000 premium autonomous SUVs starting next year. The vehicles will be built by Lucid, powered by Nuro’s Level 4 self-driving system, and managed on Uber’s platform. This program, as it says in the official announcement, was created exclusively for the Uber ride-hailing platform. So while it’s a partnership, Uber here is more of a client that ordered custom vehicles. Their exclusive robotaxis.

The reaction, of course, was instant, as this piece of news is worthy of attention. Lucid shares spiked more than 40% before the market even opened. So, what’s actually happening here? Is this a turning point for Lucid, or just the beginning of another hype cycle? What’s in it for Uber? How does it change my investing strategy? I will break it all down, because there’s a lot to unpack.

The deal

Uber is putting serious money on the table, reportedly around $300 million into Lucid, with an even larger amount expected to go into Nuro. The first batch is set to launch next year in one of the major U.S. cities, and over the next six years, the plan is to scale it to over 20,000 Lucid Gravity SUVs. These cars will be available only on the Uber platform, not sold to the public or used anywhere else.

Lucid will manufacture every Gravity specifically for this program, with the autonomous hardware built into the vehicle. After that, the cars go to Nuro, which installs its full autonomy system, the one they’ve already been running in multiple cities for years. Once they’re ready, Uber will put the vehicles to work on the platform.

I really like the deal from the point that each company is doing what it does best. It’s like going to a restaurant that serves only one main product instead of spreading to everything at once. I find these to be my favorite. Back to our topic, Lucid builds the car, Nuro installs the driver, and Uber puts it on the road. This sounds very structured and great.

Why Lucid?

Lucid is in a strange place right now. On the one hand, the technology is undeniably impressive. The Gravity SUV is a 450-mile range, 900V, ultra-fast charging machine with enough space to comfortably seat seven people. It’s premium, it’s made well, and it was already built to be an autonomous vehicle, which is the most important part. The Gravity already runs on a zonal electrical architecture, with full redundancy built in for all the major control systems (braking, steering, power, etc.). It has 360-degree perception sensors and already comes with an advanced driver-assistance setup called DreamDrive Pro, which was built with an autonomous integration idea.

So, if you thought it was a regular EV that was stuffed with L4 tech, it’s not. It was built specifically for it. And that’s still something Tesla (TSLA) hasn’t actually done. As Lucid’s interim CEO Marc Winterhoff put it, this partnership is a validation of exactly that architecture and the technology they’ve been building toward all along:

This investment from Uber further validates Lucid's fully redundant zonal architecture and highly capable platform as ideal for autonomous vehicles, and our industry-leading range and spacious well-appointed interiors, as ideal for ridesharing. This is the start of our path to extend our innovation and technology leadership into this multi-trillion-dollar market.

So while it all sounds perfect and great, let’s not forget that Lucid has some problems that did not go away. Lucid is losing a lot of money, all the time. The company just posted another $366 million loss in Q1, and deliveries, while improving, are still very tiny. The company missed the delivery expectations for June, for example.

Lucid has a reverse stock split coming, and up until now, it’s been hard to see how they were going to scale beyond a niche luxury play. The cars are impressive, but without a real strategy to reach volume, it always felt like the company was stuck in the high-end lane. Now, with this Uber partnership, the narrative around Lucid has a chance to change. Be careful here: it did not change in a day, even though the stock surged. The money problems did not go away.

Look at the numbers. There is no need to explain them. They have revenues, but the cost of revenue is more than twice as high. Just the cost of revenues. It costs them twice as much to sell each vehicle. I intentionally repeated this sentence to let it sink in. Add to it other expenses, and you get to some unbelievably high operating losses.

So, while the partnership could be potentially the beginning of a turnaround, I want to be clear. Selling 20,000 EVs to Uber for autonomous ride-hailing is very different from selling 20,000 to individual customers through a retail showroom. And there are no guarantees that anything will happen beyond the deal.

What Is Nuro

Nuro’s Driver platform is one of the few true Level 4 autonomous stacks operating without a safety driver. It’s been tested in multiple U.S. cities, passed DOT regulatory review, and runs on an AI-first architecture that’s both vehicle-agnostic and cost-optimized.

So, Nuro developed a fully driverless, already running on public roads vehicle (there is no safety driver involved, to the big surprise of Tesla investors. Yes, it’s possible). What makes Nuro stand out is that its system doesn’t need to be rebuilt every time it’s installed in a new vehicle. It was designed to be flexible. And now, for the first time, that “driver” is going into a real passenger car.

Nuro, by the way, was founded by lead engineers from Google's (GOOG) self-driving car project, which later became Waymo. Besides, it was backed by Google Ventures, SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY), and now Uber too. So while Lucid might get the attention as the carmaker and Uber as the platform, it’s Nuro that’s really making the whole thing possible. But we can’t trade Nuro, so let’s talk about Uber.

What’s in It for Uber?

Lucid stock was the one to surge, but I am excited for Uber. Quick disclaimer - I am an Uber shareholder, bought for about $65, and it’s one of three stocks I rated as a Strong Buy on this platform. I have a whole piece about Uber called “Why Driverless Cars And Tariffs Are Actually Tailwinds,” I wrote a couple of months ago.

Now, this is the most strategic move Uber has made in the autonomy space in a long time. Yes, there’s still that small partnership with Waymo, but it wasn’t anything close to transformative. That deal always had a ceiling, and we kind of hoped it would go further (it still might). But there is a good reason why the deal is limited. Waymo is part of Google, and Google doesn’t like to give up control, it’s pretty understandable. Even if the deal was to expand, Uber was never going to be the main party in that relationship. At best, it was a platform with someone else’s tech layered on top.

This is completely different. With Lucid and Nuro, Uber isn’t just plugging into someone else’s system. It’s ordering it and basically putting it together. It gets to control the pricing. It owns the customer relationship. It manages everything. Basically, Uber just killed the only strong argument the bears had about Uber’s AV presence - it doesn’t develop things. Well, now, it technically does. Not directly, of course. But Uber here is the one with the money.

And of course, it’s what Uber wants the most. In every market where Uber operates today, a big part of the money goes to drivers. And as much as Uber talks about empowering workers or improving gig economy conditions, the long-term vision was never built around human labor.

When you remove the driver from the equation, you cut your labor costs to zero and shift to a model where the car can work almost without stopping. It’s not just about cutting costs, either, it’s about keeping the vehicle working more than it could have possibly be working with a human driver. The Lucid Gravity has a longer battery range and fast charging means fewer breaks, less downtime, and more rides per day. And with Nuro’s autonomy system handling the driving, Uber no longer has to wait for Elon Musk to finally deliver the robotaxi he’s been promising for almost a decade.

So… Is This a Catalyst?

It really depends on which name you’re asking about.

For Uber, yes. This isn’t a company-defining moment yet, but it’s an important step toward building an actual AV business that it controls. The financial risk is limited because Uber’s investment is very small relative to its size, and most of the deployment cost will likely shift to third-party partners over time. If this proves to be economically viable, and that’s a big if for now, other automakers won’t stay on the sidelines for long. If Uber can show that this model works, that the cars stay on the road, that the margins hold up, and that people actually use it, more automakers will come to them offering similar deals.

For Lucid, it’s potentially transformative, but it’s way too early to say. The company has been stuck in a cycle of burn, dilute, repeat. This deal gives it a real use case and even a chance to generate recurring tech licensing revenue if things go well. But none of that changes the fact that Lucid needs to execute flawlessly. And make something else work besides this partnership. As fast as the stock surged, under specific conditions, it can drop.

And for Nuro, this is probably the most important commercial validation in its history. But we can’t invest in Nuro, so Kudos to the company, but I’ll skip this part.

Conclusion

Tesla might have the data, but it still doesn’t have a certified Level 4 system. Waymo probably has the most advanced tech on the market, but Uber is already working with Waymo. Amazon’s (AMZN) Zoox is not even in the picture, yet. Cruise is out, Apple (AAPL) walked away before it even began.

So suddenly, this partnership, Lucid, Nuro, and Uber, doesn’t feel like some moonshot anymore. It looks like Uber could potentially be one of the most powerful AV players.

Would I go buy Lucid stock just on this piece of news? No. The company still has a lot of “ifs.” For Uber, it's a serious step toward building its own AV future, not waiting around for someone else to deliver it. For Nuro, it's the move that takes them from delivery company to full passenger autonomy.

The real test starts next year, when these cars actually hit the road. This is one of the few AV efforts out there that now has the partners, the funding, and the framework to go from idea to rollout, and maybe, just maybe, rewrite the narrative on what’s actually possible.

data.title: ASML Is Still Undervalued - Time To Be Greedy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In April, I downgraded ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML, AMS: AMSL) to a Hold rating despite undervaluation due to technical weakness and a poor macroenvironment. It turns out I should have focused on the fact that it was undervalued, as the stock has gained around 12% since that article even when including the recent post earnings decline.

In the below analysis, it is shown that revenue growth remains resilient and margins are expanding. Guidance for Q3 is a bit weak but the annual outlook is still an improvement from 2024 and so long term business trajectory remains strong. Therefore, with the P/S ratio being far below its historic average, I believe ASML stock continues to be undervalued. Lastly, with a technical golden cross having just occurred recently, I believe an upgrade to a Buy is warranted.

A Lithography Milestone

Let's start today with a brief look at their cutting edge technology. Above is a diagram of their new EXE:5200B system. In their Q2, it was a major milestone when they shipped their first one to a client. As you can see, there are a variety of technological improvements, and they say this will help to "[drive] cost of technology improvement by increased productivity at better overlay performance." These better performance figures are also likely to drive the next wave of demand for the company, as they will be needed for the production of increasingly advanced AI chips.

In other news, they also introduced their NXE:3800E, showing the continued progress in litho intensity. I believe these advancements show that despite holding a dominant position in the lithography sector, the company is still willing to innovate in order to drive future growth.

Top Line Beats But Growth Is Slowing

ASML reported their 2025 Q2 earnings just days ago. They generated total net sales of 7.692 billion euros, up 23.21% YoY. Note that this is a significant slowdown in growth from the previous quarter's 46.35% growth rate. The USD figure of $8.95 billion beat expectations by $151.73 million to show robust outperformance in the quarter. As you can see above, net system sales dropped by 2.51% QoQ to 5.596 billion euros and the mix of sales also changed as well. EUV made up 48% of sales in 2025 Q2 versus 56% in previous quarter. On the other hand ArFi's share of sales increased from 33% to 43%.

In terms of regions, the portion of shipments that went to China remained steady at 27%. While the relaxation of U.S. chip control on China may alleviate risks, the fact that China continues to be a key part of ASML's business is a noteworthy item. Even though net sales of systems dropped, bookings increased by 40.78% QoQ to show that demand for their products still remains strong. In YoY terms, net system sales still increased by 17.54% to show healthy growth.

Cash Flows Drop

Despite the increase in total net sales YoY, there may be some potential concerns in their cash flows. As you can see above, operating cash flow dropped by 19.05% YoY to 748 million euros to show weakening cash generation. The great increase of cash used in financing activities is not worrying as it was due to dividend payout and share repurchases.

As an aside, ASML raised their dividend payout from 688.3 million euros to 714.4 million euros while also increasing share buybacks from just 114.9 million euros to 1.4852 billion euros. This shows a considerable jump in overall capital returns and shareholders should find that encouraging. CAPEX decreased a notable amount from the prior year period but still free cash flow dropped 17.36% YoY to $319 million. Note that these decreases in cash flows come even as EPS increased by 47.13% YoY. Therefore, the company hasn't collected a major part of their revenues, it seems.

Margin Analysis

All three of their key profitability ratios increased significantly. Gross margin increased from 51.5% to 53.7% YoY. Firstly, a gross margin above 50% is very strong. Secondly, this increase shows that pricing power has remained highly robust if not even stronger. This is another sign that demand remains strong as well. For operating margin, they reported 34.6%, up from 29.4%. While the year ago quarter's figure is no slouch, this increase demonstrates that management is continuing to find ways to boost operational efficiency. Lastly, net profit margin increased from 25.3% to 29.8%, showing that already strong profitability has become even stronger.

As a whole, these margin improvements were no surprise after seeing the fact that EPS growth far outpaced revenue growth. However, as stated above, the cash flow weakness may still be a potential concern.

Guidance

ASML provided guidance for both Q3 and the full year 2025. For Q3, at the midpoint of total net sales guidance, they are implying a growth rate of 2% YoY. This is quite a disappointment after 23% growth in their Q2. On the brighter side, the midpoint of gross margin guidance at 51% is a minor improvement from the prior year period's 50.8%. This is encouraging as it shows that at least pricing power is holding steady. R&D costs are expected to rise by 13.74%, and while that may be a drag on cash flows, I see that as a healthy increase for the development of new technologies. The rise in SG&A costs from 297 million euros to 310 million euros is more of a concern but still isn't a major issue at this point.

For the full year, they are expecting net sales growth of 15%. This is actually an acceleration from 2024's 2.56% growth rate and so the big picture for ASML is still quite bright overall. For gross margin, the are expecting 52%. This is also an improvement from the pervious year's 51.3%. This is of course also a positive development. While Q3 guidance was a bit weak, I believe this annual guidance shows that in the grand scheme of things, the business is still headed in the right direction.

Stock Is Undervalued

As you can see above, the P/S ratio is currently far below its three year average despite its recent rebound from 2025 lows. I believe its current reading of 8.810 is showing potential undervaluation. In their Q2, the company had a respectable revenue growth rate of 23.21% despite a slowdown. Furthermore, gross margin expanded significantly as another driver for P/S ratio expansion.

For Q3, revenue growth is expected to slow considerably but in the annual guidance you can see that the business is headed in the right direction in the long term. Also, gross margin is expected to expand on an annual basis as well. Therefore, I believe the P/S ratio has contracted too much from mid 2024 highs of over 15 since the fundamentals of the business remain solid at this point. In my view, investors with a long investment horizon should find this valuation to be attractive.

200 Day SMA To The Rescue

The 50 day SMA had a golden cross with the 200 day SMA back in late June to show long term bullishness. After earnings, the stock broke beneath the 50 day SMA but the 200 day SMA seems to be holding as support. Therefore, long term bulls are resilient. For the near term, it is unsurprising that there is considerable bearishness. The stock has broken below the Bollinger Bands lower band and so an oversold bounce may be due. For the MACD, the histogram shows strong downward momentum. Lastly, the RSI has plunged from nearly 70 to a current reading of just 37.81. That is quite a shift in sentiment as the bears are now in considerable control of the stock. Overall, as stated earlier, the near term is of course bearish but until the 200 day SMA gets breached, the long term outlook is still decent in my view.

Concluding Remarks

Q2 earnings were overall strong. Revenue growth was still resilient and margins expanded considerably. EPS growth was also strong but there may be some concerns about cash flows. For Q3, they are expecting a significant revenue growth deceleration but gross margin will likely stay quite steady. Furthermore, as discussed earlier, annual guidance shows that 2025 will still be a nice improvement from 2024 and so the big picture for ASML is still looking good. Therefore, I believe the P/S ratio's depressed levels show undervaluation and room for multiple expansion. Lastly, the SMAs had a golden cross recently and the 200 day SMA is holding as support to show resilient long term bulls. I believe these reasons are enough to justify an upgrade to a Buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SL Green: Stock Is In A Holding Pattern
data.description:
The last article on SL Green (NYSE:SLG) basically noted that this fiscal year was getting off to a quieter start than when management had a positive surprise multiple times in the previous fiscal year. But management hinted at an upward adjustment. The website summarized the upward guidance adjustment as well as the "beats" that happened when reporting the second quarter. The big news was an average $.40 per share FFO adjustment to guidance. This and the other positives helped the stock price to climb off its "Mr. Market worrying" lows. But it still has not approached the high point of the previous 12 months. That appears to be because the Manhattan occupancy went down (even though planned) combined with the political uncertainty that is now influencing business activity downward the longer it goes on.

Still, management reiterated that they expect the Manhattan same-store office occupancy to climb in this fiscal year. This REIT had a booming 2024 fiscal year. Business does not appear to be slowing down because there is a lull in announcements. But the trend here is admittedly very different from the typical "business office" REIT where pullbacks in this area appear to dominate as much of the industry awaits that vaunted recovery. It would appear that New York City is going to be the first to recover (or at least among the first) even if political uncertainty makes the road a bit rockier than anticipated.

The old saying about "location, location, and location" being paramount when it comes to real estate appears to be borne out here as many REIT's that led investors to believe that they had that good location are now finding out otherwise. The results of the past 18 months show the difference when a business really has control of prime locations and knows what to do with them.

Earnings Summary

It was probably a relief to the market that FFO took a step up from the first quarter.

The quarterly comparisons to the previous fiscal year are going to be tough because last year the company had so many unexpected cash flow boosts from essentially nonrecurring items. They were items that might happen with any REIT. But they were also unpredictable items that definitely helped the fiscal year along. But no one expects that level of those items in the current year.

Now there are some things management is doing to take advantage of the coming situation like starting a "debt fund" that has already purchased some distressed debt. The company may end up gaining control of some properties "on the cheap" through this fund.

However, it should be noted that buying distressed debt is a specialty all by itself as there are numerous "financial potholes" that can cause some serious losses if one is not careful. So far, things appear to be off to a good start. But the market will likely want to see a track record before assigning value to this relatively new idea.

The whole investment portfolio is a bit new and therefore needs some time for market evaluation. The company has done this to some extent before. But now there is a fund where management actually markets for money to invest.

Customer Diversification

New York City is a major money center area. So, it is consistent with that that this company has a lot of financial services business customers.

Probably the key idea is that financial services encompass a whole lot of businesses that are in different areas of the "financial services world". That could well mean that the customer list is quite a bit more diversified than a slide like this would indicate.

The company itself is largely a New York City specialist. But New York City itself has so far shown that it can outperform large parts of the country when it comes to business activity levels as far as this REIT is concerned.

That may mean that geographic diversification is not needed. Sometimes putting all the eggs in one basket and then watching that basket carefully can work as well or better than diversification. That seems to be the case here as this REIT continues to largely outperform its industry.

Summary

SL Green remains a strong buy. A stock like this probably has more upside potential as the office REIT industry continues to bottom and begin a recovery. This stock began the recovery last year with one surprise after another.

Another key is that management is growing the business. But that is good news for investors thinking that this stock should exceed its previous high price.

The stock price-to-FFO ratio is remarkably low for a company that has begun to rally from a market bottom. Usually, stocks are expensive at the beginning of the cycle and begin to look dirt cheap just before the next cyclical downturn. This one is cheap with the best yet to come.

What could make it rocky is the political uncertainty coming out of Washington D.C. The longer that uncertainty continues, the more business will hunker down to likely make any potential recession worse. Therefore, the replacement of the negotiating tools with something that is constructive and permanent is becoming more of a priority than it was when the strategy began.

So far, activity is holding up rather well. Management even raised the FFO goal. But that is likely dependent upon some certainty being restored to the business environment. Otherwise, the next six months could prove to be very rocky indeed.

This is likely a good place to consider investing if inflation rises as some predict. Real Estate nearly always keeps up with inflation. This management tends to add value as well. That could make this an interesting inflation hedge.

Risks

This REIT is a good deal more successful than much of the industry. That could change at any time with little notice for any number of reasons beyond the control of management.

The loss of services of key people could prove hard to replace. Anytime a management is outperforming the industry, the list of suitable replacement candidates is usually a small subset of the available candidates. That can mean that a loss of services could materially change the future of the company.

The current threat of tariffs and trade wars is likely a bargaining ploy that will be replaced by something that is both permanent and constructive. However, the uncertainty surrounding the bargaining is causing business activity levels to decline. This may make a recession a certainty if it continues. While this company should do well no matter what, there could be a period of stock price weakness should a recession outlook develop.

data.title: Categorizing Tech Companies' Valuation Risk
data.description:
With market valuations at such a high level today, perhaps the biggest question on the minds of investors is: Are valuations too high? The answer, of course, varies by company.

I am primarily a tech company investor, and to gain insight into this, I examined the largest tech companies by valuation and calculated their P/E Growth Ratios. I was frankly surprised by the result, which gave me pause to reconsider some of my investments.

I previously used the PEG ratio to justify NVIDIA's valuation (Nvidia: It's All About Growth), and decided to expand this analysis to compare it to others.

PEG Ratio Explanation

Some readers may not be familiar with the use of P/E growth ratios, so a brief explanation is provided below.

PEG Ratio Formula

PEG = PE / Expected EPS Growth

So if a company has a P/E of 25 and is expected to grow earnings at 15% annually, PEG=25/15=1.67

PEG Origins

The PEG ratio was created by legendary investor Peter Lynch (The PEG Ratio, Peter Lynch’s Favorite Investing Metric) for this very purpose of evaluating companies with different P/E and growth expectations:

Peter Lynch, the former Fidelity Fund manager with one of the best track records in mutual fund history, popularized the GARP style of investing while running the Magellan fund. Lynch would often look for growth companies benefiting from a market trend. Frequently, these stocks appeared expensive when viewed through a standard valuation multiple, such as the price-to-earnings ratio, but ultimately proved to be relatively cheap in terms of their growth.

To better analyze growth companies, Lynch used the PEG ratio, which is calculated by dividing the price-to-earnings ratio by the firm’s growth rate. This was one of Lynch’s favorite stock investing criteria; here's how it works:

Assume we are comparing two different stocks, both of which have a price-to-earnings ratio of 30. On the surface, they may look the same, but now let’s look at the growth rates. Let's say Company A is growing earnings at 40% while Company B is growing earnings at 15%. Company A has a PEG (price-to-earnings to growth rate) of 0.75 (30 divided by 40 equals 0.75) while Company B has a PEG of 2.0 (30 divided by 15 equals 2).

PEG Benchmark

The lower the PEG, the more attractive the company's valuation is relative to its growth rate. For Lynch, a PEG of 1.0 or less was considered very good. P/E ratios were somewhat lower at that time, so it's possible that something a little higher than 1.0 may be an appropriate benchmark.

Example: Years to Normal PE Ratio

To illustrate how this works, we can model the time it will take for the current P/E ratio to reach what is expected to be normal based on the growth rate implied by the PEG. Here is an example for a $35 stock currently trading at a 35 P/E (the median of the evaluation group below). I model four different PEG ratios, ranging from 3.0 to 1.0.

For the sake of this model, I've used 25 P/E as "normal" P/E. You could use a different target normal ratio.

The chart shows the differences in potential return at various PEGs, assuming the P/E ratio eventually reaches its normal level. A 3.0 PEG won't hit the normal P/E ratio until Year 4 and will only produce a CAGR of 1.36%, while a PEG of 1.0 reaches the normal in Year 1 and yields a CAGR of 12.35%. A PEG of 1.5 also provides a reasonable CAGR.

PEG Comparisons

When I computed the PEG for the 18 largest tech companies, I was surprised by the differences. So, I thought it would be helpful to share them.

There was a wide disparity of PEG ratios, with a median of 2.0. The median P/E ratio was 35.8, but the average was significantly higher at 52.6, primarily due to a few extremely high P/E ratios. The average and median growth rates were close at 16%-17%.

I classified them into five categories:

  1. Dark Green - These have a PEG of less than 1.5, so they have a P/E ratio reasonably balanced to their expected growth. These appear to be the most promising investment opportunities. The two with the highest P/E ratios AMD and NVDA, both have higher expected growth rates. I own all four of these stocks (including TSM and GOOGL) and will continue to hold them, possibly increasing my position.
  2. Light Green - These five companies have a higher PEG ratio but are still below the median of 2.0. Most have a reasonable P/E ratio, except AVGO, which justifies it with a higher growth rate. I own four of the five and will continue to own them, but I’ll keep an eye on them.
  3. Grey - These four stocks are in a grey area with a PEG ratio above 2.0 (the median) but below 3.0. I own all four of these stocks and will consider reducing my exposure to them in case of a market decline.
  4. Orange - With a PEG of more than 3.0, these companies are in a danger zone. I own one of them (MSFT) and will look at it more closely.
  5. Red – With a PEG greater than 8.0, I consider these stocks to have outrageous P/E ratios not justified by their growth expectations. This roughly means that they will need to grow 8X their current expected growth rates to “grow into” their current P/E ratio. I don't own either of them, but I have a short position in TSLA.

In addition to evaluating these valuations against the benchmark, it is also helpful to compare them to each other. In a comparison, you see that the first two categories may be less risky and have more upside potential than the others, particularly the high-risk companies in the red category.

Methodology

If you are interested in the calculation of the data in the table, here is an explanation:

  • All data are sourced from Seeking Alpha, including P/E ratios, market capitalizations, and growth rates.
  • The P/E ratios are forward.
  • The growth ratios represent the forward 3-5-year GAAP EPS growth rates, as projected by Seeking Alpha.
  • I choose to use the 3–5-year growth rate instead of the growth rate for the coming year. This provides a more comprehensive, long-term valuation assessment. If you use the upcoming year’s growth rate, some companies will fall below a 1.0 PEG. For example, NVDA would be 0.61 (39.7/65.0).
  • You can compute the PEG differently, such as using the current P/E without forward-looking estimates and historical growth rates. The ratios would be different, but the comparisons would be somewhat similar.
  • You can also include dividend payments in the P/E ratio, not the PEG index. Only two companies on the list have significant dividend yields: IBM (2.4%) and CSCO (2.4%). IBM's adjusted PEG would be 25.9/ (6.9 + 2.4) or 2.78. Cisco would be 17.8 / (6.3 + 2.4), or 2.04. This adjustment would make each of these a little more attractive as an investment.

Applying The PEG Ratio

You shouldn’t just take these results blindly. Instead, they should serve as a starting point in assessing valuation risk in your portfolio. The most critical factor in the equation is the assumptions for EPS growth rates. Those from Seeking Alpha are a good starting point, but you should use your own assumptions and judgment to inform your analysis. For example, if you assume a 150% annual EPS growth rate for Tesla over the next 3-5 years, you would get a more reasonable 1.2 PEG ratio.

data.title: PNC Financial Vs. U.S. Bancorp: A Dividend Investor's Dilemma
data.description:
PNC Financial Services (NYSE:PNC) and U.S. Bancorp (NYSE:USB) are top regional banks for their national reach and stability. Both are good options for dividend investors in a diversified portfolio. But if you're choosing between them, there are some key differences to consider: geographic exposure, revenue mix, and their digital offerings.

My midyear review breaks down how they’re situated looking toward year-end and offers a valuation-based verdict. I rate USB a Buy at a forward P/B of ~1.2, while PNC's recent outperformance moves it to a Hold.

Where the Business Models Diverge

Seeking Alpha’s Virtual Analyst Reports for PNC and for USB give overviews of their business models and Quant ratings. I’ll start with a quick refresher, but those already familiar may wish to skip ahead.
